content.text: Domain stewardship applies the principle that the people closest to a data asset's business meaning should govern it. In GenAI systems, this principle requires extension: new data domains emerge that have no equivalent in traditional stewardship — prompt libraries, vector embedding indexes, RLHF annotation sets, and RAG grounding corpora. Domain stewardship for GenAI assigns named stewards to each of these domains with authority and responsibility for their completeness, quality, and compliance.

The challenge is that GenAI data domains are cross-functional by nature. A RAG corpus for a customer service application may draw from the Legal domain (policy documents), the Product domain (feature specs), and the CX domain (historical support transcripts). Without explicit domain stewardship boundaries and handoff protocols, these multi-source corpora become ungoverned assemblages where no one owns quality for the combined asset.
Why It Matters

Domain stewardship for GenAI matters most at the intersection of authority and technical complexity. The engineers who build RAG pipelines understand the technical mechanics but not the business validity of the documents they index. The business domain experts understand the content but not the pipeline. A domain steward sits at that intersection: they have enough technical literacy to review pipeline configurations and enough business authority to approve or reject source documents for inclusion in grounding corpora.

Regulatory requirements reinforce this need. The EU AI Act Article 10 requires that training data for high-risk AI systems be subject to data governance practices including examination for biases and data gaps. This examination requires domain knowledge — a technical quality check on row counts and null rates is not sufficient to identify that a product domain's training data systematically under-represents a customer segment.
Benefits
Domain authority: stewards can make binding decisions about source inclusion, classification, and quality thresholds without escalation.
Business-AI alignment: domain stewards ensure that AI system behavior reflects current business definitions and policies, not stale ones.
Specialization: each domain develops deep expertise in the specific quality and compliance requirements for its GenAI assets.
Clear boundaries: when a cross-domain conflict arises (e.g., CX domain wants to include data that Legal domain considers privileged), the escalation path is explicit.
Faster onboarding: new data sources for RAG or training can be approved within the domain without central governance bottlenecks.
Trade-offs
Coordination cost: multi-domain RAG corpora require steward sign-off from multiple domains, which can slow corpus updates.
Boundary disputes: data that legitimately belongs to two domains requires a clear arbitration process, typically involving the CDO or a governance council.
Domain expertise gap: not every domain has a steward with enough AI literacy to govern GenAI-specific assets effectively — training is required.
Inconsistency: different domains may apply different quality standards to their contributions to a shared corpus, requiring cross-domain calibration.
GenAI Data Domains and Their Stewardship Requirements

attrs.alt: Domain stewardship model: four GenAI-specific domains (Prompt, Embedding, Training, RAG Corpus) governed under an Enterprise Data Governance Council with cross-domain concerns shared

content.text: Prompt Domain

System prompts, persona definitions, and prompt templates are data assets that directly shape model behavior. The Prompt Domain steward is typically from the Product or AI Safety team. They govern: version control of prompt templates, approval workflow for new system prompts, testing requirements before prompt changes reach production, and classification of prompts that embed sensitive business logic.
Embedding Domain

The vector index is the retrieval backbone for RAG systems. The Embedding Domain steward governs: embedding model selection and version pinning, chunking strategy documentation, index quality metrics (retrieval precision, recall), and the process for re-indexing when the embedding model changes. This role is typically held by a Technical or AI/ML Data Steward.
Training Domain

Fine-tuning datasets and RLHF annotation sets are the most sensitive GenAI data assets because errors compound: a biased training set produces a biased model. The Training Domain steward governs: dataset provenance documentation, consent and licensing verification for all source documents, annotation quality review, and approval before any dataset is used in a fine-tuning run.
RAG Corpus Domain

The knowledge base that grounds LLM responses at inference time. Multiple source domains typically contribute. The RAG Corpus Domain steward governs: document ingestion approval, freshness SLAs (how quickly source changes propagate to the index), document removal workflows (when a policy is superseded), and coverage measurement (what fraction of user queries can be answered from the corpus).
Mental Model: National Park Ranger Districts

Domain stewardship in GenAI is like the ranger district system in national parks. The national park headquarters sets overall policy, but each ranger district has a named district ranger who knows every trail, water source, and hazard in their zone. They have authority to close trails, approve permits, and deploy resources within their district. Central governance cannot be present everywhere; district rangers make it operational. Similarly, GenAI domain stewards make enterprise AI governance operational at the asset level.
Implementing Domain Stewardship Boundaries

# Domain stewardship registry schema
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import List, Optional, Dict

@dataclass
class GenAIDomain:
    domain_id: str
    domain_name: str  # e.g. 'prompt', 'embedding', 'training', 'rag-corpus'
    steward_name: str
    steward_email: str
    steward_team: str
    source_domains: List[str]  # business domains that contribute data
    assets: List[str]          # asset IDs in this GenAI domain
    approval_required_for: List[str]  # events requiring steward approval
    cross_domain_escalation: str  # email for boundary disputes

    def requires_approval(self, event: str) -> bool:
        return event in self.approval_required_for

    def can_accept_source(self, source_domain: str) -> bool:
        return source_domain in self.source_domains

# Example: RAG Corpus domain for a customer service application
rag_corpus_domain = GenAIDomain(
    domain_id="rag-cust-svc",
    domain_name="Customer Service RAG Corpus",
    steward_name="Maria Gonzalez",
    steward_email="maria.gonzalez@example.com",
    steward_team="CX Operations",
    source_domains=["cx", "legal", "product"],
    assets=[
        "support-kb-v3",
        "policy-docs-index",
        "product-faq-index"
    ],
    approval_required_for=[
        "new-source-domain-onboarding",
        "document-bulk-removal",
        "embedding-model-change",
        "freshness-sla-modification"
    ],
    cross_domain_escalation="governance-council@example.com"
)

# Check: does adding a Finance domain source need escalation?
source = "finance"
if not rag_corpus_domain.can_accept_source(source):
    print(f"Source domain '{source}' not approved.")
    print(f"Escalate to: {rag_corpus_domain.cross_domain_escalation}")
    # -> Escalate to: governance-council@example.com
Cross-Domain Conflict Resolution

When a RAG corpus draws from multiple source domains, conflicts arise: Legal wants certain documents classified as privileged and excluded; Product wants maximum coverage; CX wants freshness over accuracy. Resolution follows a defined process:
The domain stewards of the affected source domains negotiate bilaterally with a 5-business-day SLA.
If unresolved, the RAG Corpus Domain steward makes a provisional ruling pending escalation.
The CDO or Governance Council arbitrates within 10 business days.
The resolution is recorded in the data catalog with a rationale, effective date, and review trigger.
Examples

# Example: steward approval workflow for new source domain
import smtplib
from email.mime.text import MIMEText
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

def request_source_domain_approval(
    rag_domain: GenAIDomain,
    new_source_domain: str,
    requestor_email: str,
    sample_document_count: int
) -> dict:
    """
    Generates an approval request for onboarding a new source domain
    to a RAG corpus. Returns a request record for audit trail.
    """
    request_id = f"SRCAPPRV-{datetime.utcnow().strftime('%Y%m%d%H%M%S')}"
    deadline = datetime.utcnow() + timedelta(days=5)
    
    request = {
        "request_id": request_id,
        "rag_domain_id": rag_domain.domain_id,
        "new_source_domain": new_source_domain,
        "requestor": requestor_email,
        "steward": rag_domain.steward_email,
        "submitted_at": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        "response_deadline": deadline.isoformat(),
        "sample_document_count": sample_document_count,
        "status": "pending"
    }
    
    # In production: write to governance ticketing system, not print
    print(f"Approval request {request_id} submitted to {rag_domain.steward_email}")
    print(f"Deadline: {deadline.strftime('%Y-%m-%d')}")
    return request

# Usage
req = request_source_domain_approval(
    rag_domain=rag_corpus_domain,
    new_source_domain="finance",
    requestor_email="data-eng@example.com",
    sample_document_count=1200
)
Real-World Example

A healthcare technology company built a clinical decision support GenAI system with four contributing domains: Clinical (medical guidelines), Pharmacy (drug reference data), Regulatory (FDA approvals), and Patient Education (lay-language health content). Each domain assigned a steward: a Clinical Data Steward from the Medical Affairs team, a Pharmacy Informatics Steward, a Regulatory Affairs Steward, and a Health Communications Steward.

When a new drug received FDA approval, the Regulatory Affairs Steward triggered an update to the Regulatory domain assets within 2 hours of the public announcement. The RAG Corpus Domain steward coordinated with the Pharmacy Informatics Steward to re-index both the drug reference and regulatory sections within their 4-hour SLA. The Clinical Decision Support system reflected the new approval before clinical staff encountered the update in their workflows — a governance outcome that would have taken days without the domain stewardship structure.
Common Mistakes

Defining domains that are too broad defeats the purpose of domain stewardship. Assigning a single 'AI Data Steward' to cover all four GenAI data domains (Prompt, Embedding, Training, RAG Corpus) concentrates too much responsibility in one person and loses the domain expertise benefit. Each domain should have a steward from the relevant business function, not a single AI generalist.

A second mistake is failing to update domain boundaries as the AI system evolves. A RAG corpus that starts with two contributing source domains may expand to six as the application grows. Domain stewardship boundaries must be revisited when the corpus scope changes, and new source domain stewards must be formally onboarded with defined approval workflows.
Best Practices
Define the four GenAI data domains (Prompt, Embedding, Training, RAG Corpus) as distinct governance scopes from day one.
Assign domain stewards from the relevant business function, not only from the data engineering or AI team.
Document the list of approved source domains for each RAG corpus; require steward approval for additions.
Run cross-domain calibration sessions quarterly to align quality standards and resolve boundary ambiguities.
Include domain stewardship boundaries in the AI system's data governance documentation and model card.
Define a maximum SLA (e.g., 10 business days) for the CDO to resolve cross-domain disputes.
Performance and Scalability

Domain stewardship scales with the number of AI applications, not the number of data assets. A domain steward can govern tens or hundreds of assets within their domain with appropriate tooling. The key scalability lever is automation: automated notifications when a source document is updated, automated freshness checks against the SLA, and automated alerts when coverage metrics drop below threshold. This reduces the steward's workload to exception handling rather than routine monitoring.
Tooling and Ecosystem
Collibra Data Marketplace: domain-based asset organization with steward assignments and approval workflows.
DataHub: domain entity type supports organizing assets by GenAI domain with steward metadata.
dbt + Great Expectations: automated quality checks that can be scoped to domain-specific assertions.
LlamaIndex / LangChain: retrieval pipelines with metadata filters that enforce domain boundaries at query time.
Weights and Biases (Artifacts): dataset versioning for Training domain assets with steward approval gates.

Security and Reliability

Domain boundaries function as access control boundaries. Source documents approved by one domain steward should not automatically appear in another domain's corpus. Implement metadata-level access control on the vector index: each indexed document carries its source domain, classification, and steward approval status as metadata. At query time, the retrieval filter enforces that only approved, in-scope documents are returned. This prevents accidental cross-domain data leakage in RAG responses.

Reliability requires that domain stewards are not single points of failure. Each steward must have a documented backup with equivalent authority and system access. The backup steward's identity must be recorded in the governance registry and tested (e.g., by having the backup conduct one review per quarter) to ensure the succession plan is operational, not just documented.
Key Takeaways
GenAI introduces four new data domains requiring dedicated stewardship: Prompt, Embedding, Training, and RAG Corpus — each with distinct governance requirements.
Domain stewards must come from the relevant business function, not only from data engineering — their business authority is as important as their technical literacy.
Multi-source RAG corpora require formal cross-domain approval processes with defined SLAs and escalation paths.
Domain boundaries function as access control boundaries: only steward-approved sources should enter a domain's corpus.
Conflict resolution must be defined before conflicts arise: bilateral negotiation first, RAG corpus steward ruling second, CDO arbitration third.
Automation scales domain stewardship: stewards should handle exceptions, not routine monitoring tasks.
Each domain steward must have a documented backup who is tested in the role at least once per quarter.
